slides.title: Introduction to Docker
slides.subtitle:
Complete Containerization Guide
From Beginner to Production Ready

list.text: Usage - 65% of companies use Docker in production
Benefit - Consistent environments across development and production

box.title: Containerization platform for developing, shipping, and running applications

note.text: Fun fact: Docker gets its name from shipping containers - it packages applications like cargo containers!

list.text: Slow: Setting up new servers took hours/days.
Rigid: Difficult to scale or move between clouds.

box.title: Before Docker, developers managed a complex matrix of static dependencies and OS versions for every app/server combination.

list.text: Package once: Code, runtime, even OS dependencies.
Run everywhere: High isolation and zero conflict.

box.title: Just as shipping containers revolutionized global trade, Docker revolutionized software by standardizing the deployment unit.

list.text: Slow: Setting up new servers took hours/days.

box.title: Before Docker, developers managed a complex matrix of static dependencies and OS versions.

list.text: Package once: Code, runtime, even OS dependencies.
Run everywhere: High isolation and zero conflict.

box.title: Docker standardized the deployment unit just like shipping containers standardized cargo.

list.text: Docker Containers - Share host OS kernel, lightweight

box.title: Docker provides a management layer over existing Linux kernel features to provide isolation and resource management.

list.text: Namespaces: Provide Isolation (PID, NET, IPC, MNT, UTS).

box.title: Docker provides a management layer over existing Linux kernel features.

box.code:
# Update package index
sudo apt update

# Install prerequisites
sudo apt install apt-transport-https ca-certificates curl software-properties-common

# Add Docker's official GPG key
curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/docker-archive-keyring.gpg

# Add Docker repository
echo "deb [arch=amd64 signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/docker-archive-keyring.gpg] https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu $(lsb_release -cs) stable" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list > /dev/null

# Install Docker Engine
sudo apt update
sudo apt install docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io

# Add user to docker group
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER

# Verify installation
docker --version
box.title: Install Docker on Ubuntu

list.text: Image - Read-only template with application and dependencies
Registry - Storage and distribution system for images

box.code:
# Run a container
docker run hello-world
docker run -it ubuntu bash
docker run -d -p 80:80 nginx

# Container management
docker ps                      # List running containers
docker ps -a                   # List all containers
docker stop container_name     # Stop container
docker start container_name    # Start container
docker restart container_name  # Restart container
docker rm container_name       # Remove container

# Image management
docker images                  # List images
docker pull image_name         # Download image
docker rmi image_name          # Remove image

# Information
docker logs container_name     # View container logs
docker inspect container_name  # Detailed container info
box.title: Fundamental Docker commands

box.code:
docker system prune -a   # Remove EVERY unused object
docker volume prune       # Remove unused volumes
docker image prune        # Remove dangling images
box.title: Docker can consume a lot of disk space. Regular cleanup of dangling images, stopped containers, and unused volumes is essential.

list.text: inspect: See all JSON metadata about a container.
top: See processes running inside the container.

box.title: When a container doesn't work as expected, these commands are your best friends.

box.code:
# Sample Dockerfile
FROM node:18-alpine          # Base image
WORKDIR /app                 # Working directory
COPY package*.json ./        # Copy files
RUN npm install              # Run command during build
COPY . .                     # Copy application code
EXPOSE 3000                  # Expose port
CMD ["npm", "start"]         # Default command

# Build the image
docker build -t my-app .

# Run the container
docker run -p 3000:3000 my-app
box.title: Common Dockerfile commands

list.text: WORKDIR: Sets the working directory inside the container.
COPY: Copies new files or directories from your host to the container.
RUN: Executes commands in a new layer (e.g., npm install).

box.title: A Dockerfile is a text document that contains all the commands a user could call on the command line to assemble an image.

list.text: CMD: Sets default parameters that can be easily overridden by the user.
ENTRYPOINT: Sets the main command. Arguments passed to docker run are appended to it.
Best Practice: Use ENTRYPOINT for the executable and CMD for default arguments.

box.title: One of the most common points of confusion in Docker. Both define the process that runs when the container starts.

box.code:
# Optimization: Copy package files first
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm install

# Then copy everything else
COPY . .
CMD ["npm", "start"]
box.title: Docker uses a layer-based cache. If a layer hasn't changed, Docker reuses it. To optimize build speed, put the most frequently changed files at the BOTTOM.

box.code:
# Stage 1: Build
FROM golang:1.21-alpine AS builder
COPY . .
RUN go build -o app

# Stage 2: Production
FROM alpine:latest
COPY --from=builder /app /app
CMD ["/app"]
box.title: Multi-stage builds allow you to use multiple FROM statements in a single Dockerfile. You can compile your code in one stage and copy only the binary to a smaller production image.

box.code:
# Volume management
docker volume create my_volume     # Create volume
docker volume ls                   # List volumes
docker volume inspect my_volume    # Volume details
docker volume rm my_volume         # Remove volume

# Using volumes
docker run -v my_volume:/data ubuntu  # Mount volume
docker run -v $(pwd):/app ubuntu     # Bind mount

# Volume examples
docker run -v db_data:/var/lib/mysql mysql
docker run -v /host/path:/container/path nginx

# Clean up unused volumes
docker volume prune
box.title: Managing persistent data

slides.title: 6. Docker Storage
slides.subtitle: Persistent Data in an Ephemeral World

list.text: Volumes: Managed by Docker. Best for production. Stored in Docker's storage area.
Bind Mounts: Map a host file/directory to a container path. Best for local development.
tmpfs: Stored in memory only. Fast and secure (not persistent).

box.title: Docker provides three main ways to manage data independently of the container's lifecycle.

box.code:
# Mount a volume to persist Postgres data
docker run -v my_db_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data postgres
box.title: Since containers are ephemeral, any data stored in the writable layer is lost when the container is deleted. Storage allows us to persist databases and logs.

box.code:
# Network management
docker network ls                  # List networks
docker network create my_network   # Create network
docker network inspect my_network  # Network details
docker network rm my_network       # Remove network

# Using networks
docker run --network my_network nginx
docker run -p 8080:80 nginx        # Port mapping

# Container communication
docker run --name web --network app_network nginx
docker run --name app --network app_network my-app

# DNS resolution
# Containers can communicate using container names
box.title: Network management

list.text: Bridge (Default): Provides an internal network for containers on the same host.
Host: Removes network isolation between the container and the Docker host.
None: Disables all networking for a container.

box.title: Docker comes with several network drivers that provide different levels of isolation and connectivity.

box.code:
# Connect to database by container name
DATABASE_URL=postgres://db_container:5432/mydb
box.title: On user-defined bridge networks, Docker provides automatic service discovery via an internal DNS server. You can refer to other containers by their name instead of an IP address.

box.code:
version: '3.8'

services:
  web:
    image: nginx:alpine
    ports:
      - "80:80"
    volumes:
      - ./html:/usr/share/nginx/html
    depends_on:
      - app

  app:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@db:5432/mydb
    depends_on:
      - db

  db:
    image: postgres:13
    environment:
      - POSTGRES_DB=mydb
      - POSTGRES_USER=user
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=pass
    volumes:
      - db_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data

volumes:
  db_data:
box.title: Sample docker-compose.yml

box.code:
# Start services
docker-compose up              # Start in foreground
docker-compose up -d           # Start in background
docker-compose up --build      # Rebuild images

# Manage services
docker-compose ps              # Show running services
docker-compose logs            # View logs
docker-compose logs service_name  # Specific service logs

# Stop and cleanup
docker-compose stop            # Stop services
docker-compose down            # Stop and remove
docker-compose down -v         # Remove with volumes

# Other commands
docker-compose exec service_name command  # Execute command
docker-compose pull            # Pull latest images
docker-compose build           # Build images
box.title: Essential Compose commands

list.text: Declarative: Define the desired state in a single file.

box.title: Compose is a tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications. It uses a YAML file to configure your application’s services.

box.code:
services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports: ["8080:8080"]
    depends_on: ["db"]

  db:
    image: postgres:15
    volumes: ["db_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data"]

volumes:
  db_data:
box.title: A simple example of a web application and a database running together.

slides.title: 8. Advanced Docker
slides.subtitle: Beyond the Basics: Speed, Secrets, and Scale

list.text: Secrets: Mount secrets without leaving them in the image.
SSH Mounts: Build using your local SSH agent.

box.title: Buildkit is the next-generation build engine for Docker. It provides parallelization, smarter caching, and secret management durante build time.

list.text: Clean up: Delete caches and temp files in the same RUN command.

box.title: A smaller image is a faster and more secure image. Production containers should never contain compilers, build tools, or source code.

box.code:
# Tag images
docker tag my-app:latest myusername/my-app:latest
docker tag my-app:latest my-app:v1.0
docker tag my-app:latest registry.example.com/my-app:latest

# Push to registry
docker push myusername/my-app:latest
docker push registry.example.com/my-app:latest

# Pull from registry
docker pull myusername/my-app:latest
docker pull nginx:alpine

# Login to registry
docker login                    # Docker Hub
docker login registry.example.com  # Private registry

# Save and load images
docker save my-app > my-app.tar
docker load < my-app.tar
box.title: Image tagging and distribution

list.text: Secrets management - Use Docker secrets or environment files
Resource limits - Set CPU and memory constraints

box.code:
name: Build and Push Docker Image

on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    steps:
    - name: Checkout code
      uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
    - name: Build Docker image
      run: |
        docker build -t my-app:${{ github.sha }} .
        docker build -t my-app:latest .
        
    - name: Push to Docker Hub
      run: |
        echo "${{ secrets.DOCKER_PASSWORD }}" | docker login -u "${{ secrets.DOCKER_USERNAME }}" --password-stdin
        docker push my-app:${{ github.sha }}
        docker push my-app:latest
        
    - name: Deploy to server
      run: |
        ssh deploy@server "docker pull my-app:latest && docker-compose up -d"
box.title: GitHub Actions example

list.text: Test Stage: Run unit/integration tests in containers.
Deploy Stage: Push to registry and trigger update.

box.title: Docker is the foundation of modern CI/CD. It ensures that the exact same environment used for testing is what gets deployed to production.
Blue-Green deployment involves running two identical production environments. Only one (Blue) is live. New changes are pushed to Green, tested, and then traffic is switched.

list.text: Risk Mitigation: Detect issues early with real traffic.

box.title: Canary releases involve rolling out new features to a small subset of users before making them available to everyone.

list.text: Podman: Red Hat's drop-in replacement. No daemon required.
Finch: AWS's open-source tool for local development.

box.title: Alternatives to Docker that are often daemonless and rootless by default.

list.text: Colima: Simple CLI for running Docker/Kubernetes in VMs.

box.title: Modern Mac/Linux alternatives that offer better performance and lower resource usage than Docker Desktop.

list.text: Master Dockerfiles: Optimize for speed and security.
Learn Compose: Automate your local dev stack.
Look at Kubernetes: Prepare for massive scale.

box.title: Docker is just the beginning of your Cloud Native journey.
Dive deeper at **docs.docker.com**.
When you have hundreds or thousands of containers across many servers, manual management with docker run is impossible.

list.text: Docker Swarm: Native to Docker. Simple, easy to learn. Best for smaller stacks.
Kubernetes: The industry standard. Highly powerful, complex, and infinitely scalable.

box.title: The two dominant clustering technologies in the Docker ecosystem.

list.text: Set up volume for database persistence
Deploy and test the full stack
User Namespace: Remaps root inside container to non-root on host.
Reduced Surface: Daemon has no root privileges.

box.title: By default, the Docker daemon runs as root. Rootless mode allows running the daemon and containers as a non-privileged user, mitigating potential breakout exploits.

box.code:
# Scan an image for vulnerabilities
docker scan my-app-image
box.title: Always scan your images for known vulnerabilities (CVEs) before deploying. Docker Desktop and tools like Snyk/Trivy make this easy.

list.text: Integrity: Ensure the image hasn't been tampered with.

box.title: Docker Content Trust (DCT) allows you to use digital signatures for data sent to and received from remote Docker registries.

slides.title: 13. Common Anti-patterns
slides.subtitle: What NOT to do with Docker

list.text: Reproduction: Anyone should be able to rebuild your image.
Auditing: See exactly what is inside the image.

box.title: Avoid creating 'Golden Images' by manually installing software and 'committing' the container. Always use a Dockerfile for reproducibility.

list.text: Loss: Data will vanish when the container is removed.
Bloat: Images should remain thin and portable.

box.title: Never store persistent data (databases, user uploads) inside the container's writable layer.
Standard practice is to run exactly one main process per container. Don't try to run an entire LAMP stack in one image.

list.text: Breaking Changes: You might pull a new version accidentally.
Best Practice: Always use specific version tags or SHAs.

box.title: NEVER use the :latest tag in production Dockerfiles or Compose files. It leads to non-deterministic deployments.

list.text: Port conflicts - Check if port is already in use
Disk space - Clean up unused images and containers
Permission denied - Check user permissions and volume mounts

box.code:
# Container Management
docker run, docker ps, docker stop, docker start, docker rm

# Image Management
docker build, docker images, docker rmi, docker pull, docker push

# Network & Volumes
docker network, docker volume, docker-compose

# Information & Logs
docker logs, docker inspect, docker stats, docker exec

# System Management
docker system df, docker system prune, docker info

# Common Patterns
docker run -d -p 80:80 --name web nginx
docker exec -it container_name bash
docker-compose up -d
docker logs -f container_name

# Cleanup
docker system prune -a    # Remove all unused data
docker volume prune       # Remove unused volumes
